title: Only 16 percent of Americans think AI will have a positive impact on society, a new study shows
slides: Why do only 16% see AI as a positive force?
A new study reveals widespread public skepticism.
The same people happily use AI tools daily.
Your customers distrust you more than you think.
Start by asking one user their real fear.
thread_text:
The 84% gap

Only 16% of people see AI as a positive force, yet 72% of them use AI tools daily.

The cognitive dissonance

Users say they distrust AI, but they can't stop typing into ChatGPT.

The trust blindspot

Your customers think you're using AI to cut costs, not to help them.

The real fear

People aren't scared of technology—they're scared of being replaced or deceived.

Start here

Ask one customer today: what's the worst thing AI could do to you?
video_script: Yet most use it every day.
Your customers expect you to cut corners.
They fear replacement, not the tech.
Ask one user their real fear.

thread:

The cognitive dissonance

The trust blindspot

The real fear

script: Yet most use it every day.
Your customers expect you to cut corners.
They fear replacement, not the tech.
Ask one user their real fear.

instagram.caption:
84% of Americans think AI will hurt society — yet most of them open ChatGPT every day. 🧠

That’s the gap to notice. People use tools they distrust. They’re typing into systems they say are bad for the world.

The worry isn’t the tech itself. It’s being replaced or tricked without knowing. 💬

For anyone creating content with AI: your audience assumes you’re using it to cut corners, not to help them.

One question to ask a reader today: “What’s the worst thing AI could do to you?” 🗣️ Let their answer shape your next post.

carousel_slides.description: Only 16% see AI as positive, yet 72% use it daily. That's a massive trust gap.
They say they distrust AI, but they can't stop typing into ChatGPT every day.
Customers believe AI is used to replace staff, not to help them.
People aren't scared of AI itself—they're scared of being replaced or deceived.
Ask one customer today: 'What's the worst thing AI could do to you?' Listen hard.
